ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Ikan Singgang; mapped to closest parent class 'fish soup food product' (FOODON_03315595). Ikan Singgang is a traditional Malay clear fish broth with aromatics.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Fish soup) used as closest defensible match. No specific code exists for Ikan Singgang in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Malay fish soup preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Ikan Singgang is approximately 250 g, representing one bowl of clear fish broth with a portion of fish, as commonly served alongside white rice in a Malaysian household meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient Composition of Fish and Fish Products — IMR Malaysia MyFCD; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central (Mackerel, cooked, moist heat, FDC ID 175116) for micronutrient cross-validation
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Ikan Singgang is a traditional clear fish soup. Macronutrient and micronutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD entries for boiled/simmered fish (mackerel/kembung) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175116 (Atlantic mackerel, cooked, moist heat). Values reflect the diluted broth-plus-fish composite at approximately 55 kcal per 100 g, consistent with a clear fish soup with minimal added fat. Saturated fat estimated from USDA mackerel data proportionally adjusted for the broth dilution factor.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on clear fish broth with negligible carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ikan Singgang. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on analogy with clear fish and vegetable broths from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish contains only 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from aromatics (galangal, shallots, tamarind). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (15 × 1.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 0.45, classified as negligible. This estimate is conservative and clinically defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (180 mg per 100 g); potassium-rich fish and aromatics support blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Ikan Singgang is suitable for individuals with hypertension when prepared with controlled salt addition; the 180 mg sodium per 100 g is moderate, and the potassium content (180 mg per 100 g) from fish and galangal provides a favorable sodium-to-potassium ratio that supports vascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.2 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (estimated 15); high protein supports satiety
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ikan Singgang is an excellent choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate load (glycemic load per 250 g serving ≈ 0.45), high-quality fish protein that supports satiety and glycemic stability, and anti-inflammatory aromatics including turmeric and galangal; rice portion should be managed separately.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (1.8 g per 100 g), low saturated fat (estimated 0.5 g per 100 g), and omega-3-rich fish (mackerel/tuna) support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ikan Singgang is cardioprotective as a regular dietary component; the use of oily fish such as mackerel or skipjack tuna provides omega-3 fatty acids that reduce triglycerides and inflammation, while the low saturated fat content and absence of coconut milk make it preferable to richer Malay fish preparations for individuals managing cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory compounds from turmeric (curcumin) and galangal
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The generous use of turmeric and galangal in Ikan Singgang provides curcumin and related bioactive compounds with documented anti-inflammatory properties, making this dish a suitable regular inclusion in the diet of individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ikan Singgang is a traditional clear fish soup originating from the East Coast states of Kelantan and Terengganu in Malaysia. It is prepared by simmering whole or portioned fish — most commonly mackerel (ikan kembung), skipjack tuna (ikan tongkol), or snapper — in water with a fragrant blend of galangal (lengkuas), fresh turmeric (kunyit), garlic, shallots, and bird's eye chilies. The characteristic sour note is derived from asam keping, the dried peel of the Garcinia atroviridis fruit, which imparts a clean, tangy acidity to the broth without the richness of tamarind paste. Unlike the majority of Malay fish dishes, Ikan Singgang contains no coconut milk and no heavy rempah (spice paste), resulting in a light, translucent, golden-hued broth with a savory-sour flavor profile and a delicate, aromatic finish. Nutritionally, Ikan Singgang is a high-protein, low-calorie, low-fat dish providing approximately 55 kcal per 100 g, with 8.5 g protein and only 1.8 g fat. The carbohydrate content is negligible at 1.2 g per 100 g, giving it a very low glycemic index (estimated 15) and a glycemic load of less than 0.5 per standard serving. It is rich in vitamin B12 (2.2 mcg per 100 g), niacin (3.8 mg), selenium (15 mcg), and phosphorus (95 mg), reflecting the nutritional profile of oily fish. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The anti-inflammatory properties of turmeric and galangal also make it beneficial for inflammatory conditions. Ikan Singgang is traditionally served with steamed white rice and fresh ulam (raw herb salad) and is considered a staple home-cooked meal and a restorative dish for those recovering from illness.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Ikan Singgang?
Is Ikan Singgang good for diabetes?
What fish is used in Ikan Singgang?
Is Ikan Singgang the same as Pindang Ikan?
What is asam keping in Ikan Singgang?
meal_context: served with white rice and ulam

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD entries for boiled fish (mackerel/kembung) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175116. No single approved-source entry exists specifically for Ikan Singgang as a composite dish; values represent a defensible estimate for the broth-plus-fish composite. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for clear fish broths. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of a direct exact-match entry in any approved source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD (boiled mackerel/kembung) and USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175116 (Atlantic mackerel, cooked, moist heat). No exact Ikan Singgang composite entry exists in any approved source; values represent a defensible broth-diluted estimate consistent with 55 kcal per 100 g. 2. Saturated fat estimated at 0.5 g per 100 g based on proportional adjustment of USDA mackerel saturated fat data for broth dilution; this is an approximation. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for this traditional composite preparation are not available in IMR MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central as a dish-level entry as of April 2026. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Ikan Singgang exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The estimate is based on clear fish broth analogues and is clinically conservative given the negligible carbohydrate content. 5. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315595 and FoodEx2 code A0FJP are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for Ikan Singgang. 6. This is a Malaysian dish; multilingual requirements apply for Indonesian (satisfied: Pindang Ikan) and Malay (satisfied: Ikan Singgang). Chinese, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable for this Malaysian East Coast Malay dish and are set to null. 7. The input source citation 'Nutrient Composition of Traditional East Coast Malaysian Fish Soups, Health Promotion Board Singapore' could not be verified as a specific HPB publication; nutrient provenance has been re-anchored to IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central as approved primary sources.
